data.title: RXO, Inc.: Stock Likely To Remain Range-Bound Until Demand Recovers
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on RXO, Inc. (RXO) in August, in which I recommended a hold rating as the overall freight market was still weak with no immediate catalyst to drive a recovery, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I stay neutral for RXO. The structural capacity exits are finally real, which supports the long-term setup, and RXO continues to execute very well on the Coyote integration. But none of this offsets the near-term squeeze, in that margins are getting hit by rising buy rates, contract repricing hasn’t started, and demand is still soft. Until volumes turn, the stock is at best going to stay range-bound.

Structural Capacity Exits Are Finally Real

The most bullish development in the last few months is that the supply side of the freight market is finally correcting, and I mean in a very meaningful way. Over the years I have covered RXO on Seeking Alpha; this capacity issue has been the biggest overhang on the industry. We keep hearing about capacity leaving the system, but it was hard to believe that this is the case since spot rates did not reflect it. Per management during the Q3 call, there was just too much supply relative to demand. But I think there are now even better data points to convince the market that capacity is indeed leaving the industry. Specifically, the tighter enforcement around non-domiciled commercial driving licenses [CDLs] and English-proficiency requirements is driving real capacity exits. The FMCSA has noted ~200,000 non-domiciled CDLs as at risk, which is about 5% of total capacity (Source: Q3 transcript), and California alone revoked 17,000 licenses. The DOT has even found 44% of ~16,000 trucking schools to be non-compliant. If they do not comply within 30 days (end of this year), a chunk of new supply will no longer flow into the system.

I am increasingly inclined to think that a structural reset is going to happen, and not a typical cyclical one. If we look at the previous downturns, drivers (i.e., the supply) could exit the industry when rates are poor and re-enter when demand conditions improve. This time is different. Many of these drivers are potentially losing the legal right to operate, and that removes the uncertainty that these drivers could re-enter the supply pool. Now, this does not mean we swing back into a full recovery mode yet, but I think the implication is straightforward. Once demand eventually turns (like always, it will recover at some point), there won’t be as much capacity to absorb the surge in volume, and that should drive up rates and volume, which sets the stage for RXO to outperform.

Coyote Integration

RXO continues to surprise me on the Coyote integration side of things. As a recap, I was bullish on this aspect previously, as there was solid evidence that the incremental scale advantage was benefiting the P&L. The good news is that management has explicitly noted that the integration is “materially done” and ahead of schedule. The carrier-side tech stack was integrated in May, and the shipper side is now largely migrated.

As of Q3, RXO is running on a single CRM, ERP, and pricing system, and I have two takeaway points here. One is that this removes a major overhang that this integration could divert management’s attention. Two, this has significantly simplified operations, so we should start to see an even more apparent scale advantage (in terms of growth and margins). So far, RXO has already captured $60 million in annualized operating expense synergies, and with the combined data set now feeding directly into their pricing models, we should see better pricing accuracy (i.e., better yields) as volumes scale.

The Squeeze Is Here, and It Is Painful

Now for the bad news. The same structural capacity reduction that I discussed above is going to hurt the near-term P&L. As much as the long-term benefits are sound, the sad fact is that ~70% of RXO’s truckload exposure (Source: Q3 call) is under contract, and those rates were set back when there was overcapacity. Now that the buy rates (amount RXO pays to third-party truck drivers) are starting to increase (noted to have moved higher in September, with spot rates going up as well, RXO is effectively absorbing the cost increase while its contract pricing stays flat. This “squeeze” is well seen in brokerage gross margin, which fell to 13.5% in Q3, down 90 bps sequentially, and October truckload gross profit per load was ~25% below the five-year average.

Management even made it clear that this squeeze will get worse in Q4, and they have guided for it: brokerage gross margins to fall to 12%–13%. Remember that the bid season only progresses from October through February (Source: GS conference). So, until then, RXO will continue to be squeezed, and potentially squeezed even more if spot rates continue to tighten. This would weigh on the next one or two quarters of earnings.

Still no signs of demand recovery

The biggest problem that is still weighing on the stock is, I believe, the lack of a demand catalyst. Management continues to describe the demand environment as a prolonged soft freight market and even noted that trends weakened through Q3. With truckload volumes down 11% y/y (for Q3), there is just not much a broker like RXO can do to drive growth.

In a normal market recovery, RXO will do well when freight demand exceeds shippers' primary carrier lists (also known as routing guides). When those guides fail (everyone on the contract list rejected the load), shippers are forced to pay premium prices for last-minute loads, and that is a lucrative volume stream for RXO (premium price = higher margin). That is not happening today. Demand is so weak that shippers' primary carriers can handle all the volume, so no high-margin volume is routing over to brokers (management confirmed this by saying there was no meaningful corresponding increase in accretive spot opportunities).

Ultimately, if demand doesn’t recover, the structural reset in capacity is unlikely to shift the narrative to bullish, in my view.

Valuation

The valuation picture is tricky. On the next twelve months' numbers, RXO looks expensive because earnings have collapsed to such a low level (LTM adj. EPS is negative, for context). But if we look at the two-year forward P/E, RXO trades just slightly above its historical average, which is not really expensive. If you believe, as I do, that the supply removal is real, then 2026 earnings power could be significantly higher than 2025. But with EBITDA running at a ~$100 million annualized rate in Q4 (vs. a market cap of ~$2.3B and EV of ~$3B), you are really paying a massive multiple for that future recovery. If it works, great, but I think the risk/reward is not very attractive, as the stock is effectively pricing in a V-shaped recovery that hasn't started yet. I prefer to wait until we see evidence that contract rates are actually moving higher to justify this multiple.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for RXO. The structural story is getting better, in that capacity is leaving, and Coyote is integrated. But the timing is still unfavorable. Contract rates won’t reset until bid season progresses, spot tightness is hitting margins today, and there’s no sign of a demand recovery. With the stock already pricing in a rebound, I don’t think the current risk-reward is compelling.

data.title: Coca-Cola Europacific Partners: Risk-Reward Setup Remains Unattractive
data.description:
Investment Action

I had a hold rating on Coca-Cola Europacific Partners (CCEP) earlier because I felt the upside was limited. My current view is still neutral (but more cautious). On the one hand, the execution in energy and away-from-home continues to outperform. On the other hand, the October slowdown and softer scanner data suggest the European consumers are not spending as much. Paired with a premium valuation, I think the risk-reward setup remains unattractive.

Some Positive Points

I want to start by acknowledging that the bulls are right because CCEP is executing very well in the high-value categories I highlighted previously. Energy remains the main key driver here, which I have noted in my earlier note. Just for recap, this category saw 24% y/y volume growth in the latest Q3 quarter, which was a massive acceleration from 15% y/y in 1H 2025. Two bullish points here. The first is that this growth is a structural move, as it is driven by product launches like Lando Norris and Ultra Vice, which means CCEP could theoretically repeat this by innovating more products. The second is it shows that there is a real mix shift toward higher-value units. Remember that these single-serve formats have better economics than multi-serve units, so the increase in mix here should directly have a margin uplift impact.

CCEP's effort to drive demand in Europe has also seen some good traction. Away-from-home volumes grew 0.7% y/y in the latest quarter, driven by the new wins in quick-service restaurants [QSR] and coffee shops. I see this as a significant win because the away-from-home channel typically carries higher margins than the home channel, and more importantly, growing volumes in this channel while the broader European consumer spending is weak validates the narrative that this channel is more "sticky." Then if we add on the fact that CCEP will see a record level of cooler placements this year (per management expectations), it sets up really well for more volume growth in 2026. For those that are unaware, these placements are used for increasing distribution in the away-from-home channel, which means more distribution capacity to capture demand.

Softening Demand and Valuation Disconnect

However, these bright spots in the story don't change the fact that the macro tone has turned for the worse, and the October slowdown is hard to ignore. Scanner data for the four weeks ending November 2 shows Western Europe volumes down 0.7% y/y, with Germany falling 2.2% and the UK down 2.5%. The grocery channel also looks like it is finally feeling the pain of inflation (note that away-from-home demand was up in Q3; if that stays true, it implies food-at-home, or groceries, is down by a lot). The line in the Q3 transcript that led me to this conclusion is when Damian said they saw "softening in demand as affordability and value for money become increasingly relevant drivers for more consumers." He even went one step ahead to say the amount of products bought is declining (not the purchase frequency). This additional data point plus the scanner data paints a weak demand picture. Here is the worry. If December behaves the same way, which management called out as the key month for Q4, then we are not going to see a strong near-term demand bounce, in my view.

The drag in Asia Pacific complicates the outlook. The Philippines has turned around after the typhoon-driven disruption, with volumes returning to growth in September and improving again through October and November. However, Australia is still dealing with the Beam Suntory distribution exit. This is not new to the market, but the timing matters. The impact will be felt through 1H2026, and that would weigh on the overall headlines, especially when Europe is not doing well.

From a technical perspective, the next few quarters are going to be noisy, and for most investors, I suspect they will just wait for cleaner numbers before they turn more bullish. Which also means the stock is unlikely to see a sharp upward revision in valuation. One fortunate thing here is that CCEP is onboarding Bacardi, which should drive some momentum, but even here, management has been clear that it won't meaningfully close the demand weakness in the near term.

The consensus organic sales growth estimate sits at 3.2% y/y (this is below CCEP's mid-term algorithm of 4%), and I don't see any immediate catalyst for a revision in estimates. Without a clear macro rebound, and given the October scanner data, it's hard to pinpoint where the upside surprise would come from. Some of the bulls would cite the 2026 FIFA World Cup as a catalyst, but my view is that it usually serves more as a brand marketing event rather than one that drives major volume growth.

Despite this visible weakness, you would be surprised to learn that CCEP is actually trading at its 4-year NTM PE of 18x and is also the +1 standard deviation over the past 10 years. I don't really get the bullishness here. It seems that the market is implying a broader macro recovery going into next year, which is really up to anyone's guess. On a relative basis, the disconnect is also huge. CCEP has guided for mid-single-digit revenue growth next year, which is much lower than Royal Unibrew, but CCEP actually trades at a premium (18x vs. 16.5x).

The risk/reward setup here simply isn't amazing. If macro continues to stay weak and CCEP fails to deliver any surprise, valuation could very quickly see a huge de-rating to reflect the "fair" multiple. While this is not the best comp, Diageo Plc (another beverage player in Europe), which has a low-single-digit growth outlook, trades at 14x NTM PE, so this could be the downside we are looking at.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating. CCEP is executing well in its highest-value categories, and the away-from-home momentum should carry into 2026. But the demand softness in Europe, the drag in Asia Pacific, and a premium valuation limit the upside. Unless the macro turns quickly or the Bacardi transition ramps faster than expected, the risk-reward setup doesn't look amazing to me.

data.title: TSI: Time To Take A Bite Out Of This CEF
data.description:
Thesis

Coming from the TCW Group, the TCW Strategic Income (TSI) CEF is a name we have covered in the past on the Seeking Alpha platform. In our last article from September 2024, we downgraded the CEF to 'Hold', arguing that valuations were not cheap. The fund is fairly flat since, with a lower price level but a slightly positive total return.

This year, we have seen the CEF move to a larger-than-normal discount to NAV, prompting us to revisit the fund.

Current portfolio composition

Let us take a look at how the CEF's portfolio is shaping up currently:

The fund is still overweight MBS securities, which make up over 47% of the holdings. The split is almost even between Agency MBS and Non-Agency MBS. Please remember Agency MBS bonds are rated AAA and have no credit risk, while Non-Agency MBS do carry a risk of default.

The CEF has a large 22% bucket for corporates, which includes municipal credits. ABS make up 16% of the fund, followed by CMBS at 10%.

From a ratings perspective, the CEF is overweight investment-grade credits:

Below investment grade holdings account for only 30% of the fund, albeit the CCC bucket is rather large here. As discussed above, Agency MBS are 24% of the fund and compose most of the AAA assets. The rest can be ABS or CMBS securities. The second largest investment-grade bucket is made up of BBB credits, which account for 19% of the fund. The below-investment-grade bucket is split fairly evenly among BB, B, and CCC credits.

This CEF is very active in managing its holdings, with a reported 12-month turnover rate for its portfolio of 244%. This means the manager is always looking for attractive risk/reward opportunities in the market.

Some fund analytics

Let us also take a look at some analytics for this CEF:

  • AUM: $250 million.
  • Discount to NAV: -6%.
  • z-stat: -1.8.
  • Distribution: 5.3%.
  • Distribution frequency: Quarterly.
  • Duration: 2.7 years.
  • Leverage: 0%.

The name is a fairly conservative CEF with a low duration and no leverage currently (although it can ramp that up to 33%). We can see the fund trading at a discount to NAV, a wider discount than usual as shown in the z-stat.

You will also notice something interesting when it comes to the CEF's distribution: it is only 5.3%. This fund does not try to market itself via a very high and unsustainable yield. As we shall see from its performance section the name does generate total returns around 7% on average, with the difference coming from capital gains. We like this feature.

Premium/Discount to NAV

In the past few years the CEF has been trading in a range of -8% to 0%:

We can see the fund moving from flat to NAV to an -8% discount once rates moved much higher in 2022. The name had periods of mean reversion, narrowing its discount above the -4% average. It is now back below that at -6%.

Performance

The CEF has done well when rates are moving lower:

  • 2019: +17%.
  • 2020: +3.7%.
  • 2021: +8.0%.
  • 2022: -14%.
  • 2023: +7.0%.
  • 2024: +13.3%.
  • YTD: +6.4%.

We can see the CEF up the most during those years when rates moved lower, like 2019 or 2024 (a large boost towards the end of the year there).

As we saw from the composition section, this CEF is not leveraged and overweight MBS securities. Thus, we can compare it with pure MBS funds:

We are comparing the CEF with the First Trust Low Duration Opportunities ETF (LMBS), with the Angel Oak Income ETF (CARY), and the iShares MBS ETF (MBB). We can see from the above graph that all securities have had a similar performance in the past year, albeit TSI did outperform for a long stretch (the teal line is above its competitors for most of the year). Until the discount to NAV widened recently, the CEF was outperforming.

Let us see the same graph on a longer time frame:

Over a longer time frame, TSI is the clear winner, and we attribute this performance to its active management and large sleeve of non-MBS securities. We can also see a very clear differentiation among MBS-only funds, with CARY also an outperformer. We already have a 'Buy' rating next to CARY, with our article present on the Seeking Alpha platform.

Distributions are not a marketing tool

What we like about this CEF is its ability to distribute what it makes, rather than use that as a marketing tool. The current 5.3% distribution is fully supported as per the latest Section 19a notice:

Investors are basically buying into a fund that offers a distribution equivalent to the portfolio cash yield, layered on top with capital gains generated from the trading acumen of the portfolio manager and the positioning versus the market.

Given the fund's duration of roughly 3 years and the current market pricing of 100 bps in Fed Fund cuts in the next year, the implied total return here on a 12-month basis is roughly 8%. TSI, however, is more volatile than regular MBS ETFs, with an annualized volatility close to 9%.

We are therefore bullish on this name, given the valuation band in its discount to NAV and its targeted approach to the yield curve, which is a take on the front end. If the market expectations do materialize, the name should deliver a robust return, albeit with a sizable volatility for the asset class.

Conclusion

TSI is a fixed-income closed-end fund. The CEF has no leverage but does hold the ability to run its gearing ratio up to 33%. The fund does not use marketing gimmicks, with a passthrough distribution of only 5.3%. TSI does best when rates move lower via its duration profile of 3 years and overweight MBS holdings. The CEF's discount to NAV has widened out recently, moving towards the bottom of its historic range. With the Fed set to cut significantly next year, we are penciling in an 8% total return here on a 12-month horizon, thus assigning TSI a 'Buy' rating.

data.title: Whale's Methodology: Institutional Trading Mindset (1)
data.description:
Originally published on November 6, 2025

For most investors, the question "What is trading?" is one they've never seriously considered. Many see trading as a means to "get rich overnight," while others equate it with "gambling." In reality, the "trading" understood by retail investors and the "trading" understood by institutional investors are completely different things, and this is the key reason for the difference in investment results.

In financial markets, the performance of retail investors and institutional investors often presents a stark contrast. According to multiple studies, over 70% of retail investors fail to outperform the market in short-term trading and even incur losses of principal. In contrast, institutional investors, such as hedge funds, pension funds, and banks, typically achieve relatively stable and consistent returns. This gap is not accidental but stems from multiple factors, including behavioural psychology, information resources, strategy execution, and market structure.

Decades ago, the gap between individual and institutional investors was almost "insurmountable." Institutional investors possessed sufficient information asymmetry, gaining access to information days earlier than retail investors. They could also borrow large sums of money or seek powerful partners to influence or even manipulate the market. In contrast, retail investors cannot use derivatives and must place orders through brokers when buying and selling stocks, incurring significant transaction costs; as a result, day trading is virtually impossible.

However, in the 2020s, thanks to the development of the internet and blockchain technology, everything changed. Retail investors can easily access tools like options, using leverage to beat short-selling institutions. Retired legendary traders share their trading strategies in communities, leading investors to previously unattainable returns. Algorithmic trading is no longer the exclusive domain of hedge funds and market makers, and real-time data and charts are readily available on any device. Clearly, the difference between institutions and retail investors has narrowed significantly, and in some areas (such as arbitrage trading), it is now merely a difference in scale.

Therefore, while retail investors still cannot match the vast capital of institutions, the difference between them stems more from differences in methodology, trading methods, and risk management than from differences in transaction costs or information. However, it is precisely these different methodologies, trading methods, and risk management approaches that lead to different investment results for retail and institutional investors in most situations.

In this series of articles, we will take an institutional perspective to "reshape" the investment mindset of retail investors, combining institutional trading thinking and methodologies with daily investment to help investors build the right trading logic and a robust investment framework.

What Is Trading?

Trading refers to the process of buying and selling various assets in financial markets, with the intention of profiting from price fluctuations or hedging risks. These assets include stocks, bonds, foreign exchange, commodities, cryptocurrencies, and derivatives (such as futures and options).

The above definition comes from university classrooms and textbooks. In practice, institutional trading primarily aims to "obtain stable excess returns," that is, to achieve returns exceeding those of risk-free assets, such as government bonds and cash, while keeping risks under control. The reason is simple: one of the primary sources of income for financial institutions, such as banks, is the interest rate spread. Without excess returns, after inflation correction, the interest rate spread may not be able to withstand inflation's erosive effects. For retail investors, this is also one of the main purposes of trading: to hedge against asset devaluation caused by inflation.

On the other hand, "hedging risk" itself may also be a purpose of trading. Many companies participate in futures and options trading to hedge against risks associated with inventory price fluctuations and ensure stable cash flow. With the gradual democratisation of derivatives, retail investors can also use derivatives to hedge some of the higher-risk exposures in their portfolios, such as cryptocurrencies and small-cap stocks.

It is not difficult to see that the core of trading is not speculation, but rather the management and effective allocation of risk. In this process, investors choose to bear certain risks and opportunity costs in exchange for potential portfolio performance optimisation and additional returns.

Of course, speculative elements also exist in trading, but they typically occur after additional returns have already been achieved. When there's a surplus, many people will use the money meant for chewing gum or chocolate to buy a lottery ticket. Similarly, for institutions, using a portion of surplus funds for speculative trading is nothing new - it can even be seen as a potential means of enhancing returns: even if these funds are completely lost, it won't significantly impact this year's revenue, while a successful bet can "add icing on the cake" to portfolio performance.

However, it must be acknowledged that in most cases, the stable returns of institutions do not come from those bets. So, where do the excess returns of institutions come from?

Source of Trading Profits: Implicit Subsidies

Implicit subsidies are one of the magic wands institutions use to generate trading profits. In financial markets, implicit subsidies are a unique phenomenon referring to premiums paid for trades that are not for traditional risk-return optimisation purposes. Unlike regular risk premiums, implicit subsidies arise from market inefficiencies, offering investors stable trading opportunities and returns.

To some extent, implicit subsidies can be viewed as a service fee. In the derivatives industry, implicit subsidies are referred to as "convenience yields," which represent the costs incurred in holding derivative contracts. A typical example is BTC ETF issuers: after acquiring BTC spot, they open short positions in BTC futures on the CME, obtaining convenience yields while hedging against price volatility risk. Convenience yields depend on the supply and demand of the contract, not the price, and are consistently positive over the long term, providing institutions with continuous and stable returns.

In fact, implicit subsidies are widespread in financial markets. Government and central bank interventions, or commitments to intervene, are common sources of implicit subsidies. Issuers of emerging market bonds typically pay a premium to investors to compensate for potential liquidity risks and higher default risks compared to more developed markets. During periods of significant price volatility, risk-averse investors are often willing to pay a higher premium to buy options or other derivatives to protect their positions, or pay substantial slippage to liquidate their holdings.

Based on these market participants' needs, institutional investors can obtain implicit subsidies from multiple sources: banks can receive subsidies from central banks by providing liquidity in the repo market, fixed-income traders can obtain subsidies from the bond market through curve trading, and hedge funds and market makers can obtain subsidies by selling volatility and getting slippages—this is the secret to their long-term profitability. It's important to note that these sources of profit are not significantly related to the performance of the asset price itself. This is also a point that retail traders need to learn: directional trading does not guarantee long-term, stable returns.

Clearly, institutional trading profits are not limited to implicit subsidies; this will be discussed in subsequent chapters.

Stay tuned…

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Personalized Investment Models For Retirement Plans And IRAs
data.description:
Yaqub Ahmed is Global Head of Retirement, Workplace & Wealth with the FIRST Organization (Franklin Innovation Research Strategies Technology). In 5 Trends Shaping the Future of Retirement he explains:

The industry created somewhat of a monster with target-date funds, with its "set it and forget it" mindset, thereby limiting participant engagement.

"Some may argue that it's a better approach, but we believe participants are far from disengaged," Ahmed responded. "They want to be involved—they just don't know where to start. The real issue is that we're not effectively activating those engagement levers.

Target date funds (TDFs) are crying for improvements that are just beginning. This article is about contemporary models that enable personalization. These improvements apply to both retirement savings plans and IRAs, in other words, to all tax-deferred savings. And most of all, this article is about substantive prudence—doing what is best and right.

Better models

Investment models are ubiquitous. Investors can get models from just about any money manager, using that manager's funds, of course.

I present a framework for investment models that integrates lifetime investment paths—like those used in target date funds—with academic lifetime investing theory.

To keep things simple, I use just two assets—a well-diversified portfolio of risky assets and a safe asset like T-bills. Ibbotson Associates labeled this approach the "Separation Principle." Identify the model that is best for you and implement it with your favorite risky and safe portfolios, potentially with the help of an investment advisor.

Framework

My models are a sequence of target risk models along the efficient investment frontier, where your position on the frontier is a function of your age and your risk tolerance, as shown in the following:

Academic lifetime investment theory sets the risk level based on age. It integrates human capital with investment capital as follows:

As our human capital depletes, we become increasingly dependent on our investments, so theory says that our investments can be risky when we're young, but we should become less risky as we age. As we are near retirement, theory says very low risk, as shown in the following:

But we all have our personal feelings about risk that create our risk preferences. Behavioral scientists tell us that we are risk-averse—we don't like risk. But some people are risk takers, and they want their money to work really hard. Academic theory sets the standard for the risk-averse majority, but an individual can override theory. Hence, the red ages in the efficient frontier graph above.

This theory brings us up to retirement, at which time I point to a different theory.

Investing in retirement

The academic theory for investing in retirement was introduced in Kitces and Pfau's 2013 paper on Reducing Retirement Risk with a Rising Equity Glide-Path. Kitces & Pfau make a very persuasive case for entering retirement with low risk and then re-risking in retirement. We all pass through 3 stages of investing where the second stage of transitioning from working life to retirement should be very safely invested.

Connecting with pre-retirement theory, the pre-retirement glidepath needs to end safely to connect with a post-retirement path that starts safely, creating a U shape.

Implementation

Acknowledging different levels of risk tolerance requires several lifetime glidepaths as shown in the following:

Investors can blend these glidepaths and they can change their risk tolerance at will. And there you have a framework for investment models. Voila!

How do one-size-fits-all TDFs fit in this model?

TDFs are all very similar to one another and fit into my model framework as shown in the following:

As shown in the exhibit, TDFs take moderate risk until they reach the Risk Zone when they become high risk, and then in retirement they become low risk. Note that participants in the Risk Zone are taking a high risk. They don't know it, but they will when the stock market crashes. A crash will be a "good" thing because it will expose this high risk, opening the door for improvements. Substantive prudence will morph into procedural prudence.

Conclusion

Albert Einstein said that everything should be as simple as possible, but no simpler. Here is a simple universal investment framework. Please use it.

Readers of this publication expect an action step. How should you use this article? Here's what you can do:

  1. Think about your risk tolerance. Most of us have low risk tolerance, but you might be different.
  2. Find your risk tolerance path (low, middle, or high risk) in the last graph above and locate yourself on the horizontal axis by finding the number of years that you are away from retirement, and then look diagonally to your left for an allocation to your diversified risky portfolio.
  3. Act.
    1. Is the answer near your current portfolio? This will either confirm or question what you're currently doing.
    2. If you're invested in a target date fund, how close is your answer to a TDF? If it's not close, consider getting out of your TDF, especially if it's riskier than your answer.

data.title: Gold Outlook 2026: Push Ahead Or Pull Back
data.description:
Push ahead or pull back

Gold has experienced a remarkable 2025, achieving over 50 all-time highs and returning over 60%.1 This performance has been supported by a combination of heightened geopolitical and economic uncertainty, a weaker US dollar, and positive price momentum. Both investors and central banks have increased their allocations to gold, seeking diversification and stability.

Looking to 2026, the outlook is shaped by ongoing geoeconomic uncertainty. The gold price broadly reflects macroeconomic consensus expectations and may remain range-bound if current conditions persist. However, taking cues from this year, 2026 will likely continue to surprise. If economic growth slows and interest rates fall further, gold could see moderate gains. In a more severe downturn marked by rising global risks, gold could perform strongly. Conversely, a successful outcome from policies set by the Trump administration would accelerate economic growth and reduce geopolitical risk, leading to higher rates and a stronger US dollar, pushing gold lower.

Additional factors, such as central bank demand and gold recycling trends, could also influence the market. Most importantly, gold’s role as a portfolio diversifier and source of stability remains key amid continued market volatility.

Chart 1: Continued market volatility and geoeconomic risk could push gold higher, but a reduction in risk premia could put pressure on its performance

2026 implied gold performance based on hypothetical macroeconomic scenarios*

Gold’s impressive surge

After setting more than 50 all-time highs and edging over 60% by the end of November, gold has emerged as one of the strongest performing assets in 2025 (Chart 2).

This historic rally, gearing up to be gold’s fourth strongest annual return since 1971,2 has been driven by a combination of factors.

At a macro level, two stand out:

  • A supercharged geopolitical and geoeconomic environment
  • Generalised US dollar weakness and marginally lower rates.

This environment has resulted in a broader push for portfolio diversification amid lacklustre bond returns and concerns of frothiness in equity markets.

Against this backdrop and further supported by gold’s positive momentum, investment demand has surged across all regions from West to East.

At the same time, central banks continued their buying spree – with demand well above average, even if below the records seen in the previous three years.

Chart 2: Gold takes a place on the podium in 2025

Y-t-d returns for gold and key asset classes in USD*

Our Gold Return Attribution Model (GRAM) – summarised in Chart 3 and Table 1 – indicates that the high-risk environment explains roughly 12 percentage points of gold’s y-t-d return, primarily driven by geopolitical risk. Reduced opportunity cost – through a weaker US dollar and marginally lower rates – contributed another 10 percentage points.

Within the two factors above, the combined effect of heightened geopolitical risk and US dollar weakness accounted for roughly 16 percentage points. This underscores the outsized influence of politics and macro uncertainty on gold’s performance so far during Trump’s second term.

Further, price momentum and investor positioning contributed nine percentage points, while economic growth added 10 points.

Chart 3: Geopolitical risk, dollar weakness, and investment flows drove gold higher

Key drivers of gold’s return by month via GRAM*

Notably, the contributions of the four main factors that drive gold have been unusually balanced this year (Table 1). This signals a market driven by diverse forces rather than a single catalyst. Having said that, momentum has played a larger role than in previous years, which is not surprising considering how gold’s strong rally has prompted widespread investor interest.

Table 1: Gold was driven in almost equal measure by its four key drivers

Return contribution from key gold drivers by year (%)*

Category Metric 2025 2024 2023
Risk & uncertainty GPR risk 8 3 1
Other 4 2 2
Opportunity cost FX risk 8 5 2
Other 2 2 2
Momentum 9 3 2
Economic expansion 10 5 3
Total above 41 20 12
Other factors** 20 6 3
Gold total return 61 26 15

*Data as of 28 November 2025. Numbers may not add up to total due to rounding. Figures are shown as a % and represent the percent contribution to gold’s return by year. For more information on our Gold Return Attribution Model (GRAM) please see Chart 3.

**Other factors not included in the model, due to data availability (such as central bank purchases), transient effects, or analysis limitations.

Source: World Gold Council

What to expect in 2026

Looking ahead to 2026, markets are largely pricing in a continuation of the status quo, but divergences in macro data laden with a heavy geoeconomic blanket mean that uncertainty will remain high. Concerns about a softening US labour market are mounting, while debates persist over whether inflation will stay stubbornly high or face renewed upward pressure. At the same time, and despite some progress, geopolitical frictions continue to simmer.

What does this mean for gold? Much like this year, unforeseen events – such as Liberation Day – are impossible to anticipate. Still, while their exact nature is unpredictable, the frequency of tail risk events is on the rise (Chart 4).3 Whether such developments trigger risk-on or risk-off sentiment could play a decisive role in shaping performance across asset classes and gold’s role as a strategic diversifier.

Chart 4: Tail-risk events are on the rise

S&P 500 kurtosis and skew index*

What macro consensus tells us

The gold price today largely reflects macro consensus expectations related to economic growth, inflation, and monetary policy.4

This is captured by the range-bound performance shown by our Gold Valuation Framework when we input market consensus variables (Table 2). Namely:

  • Global GDP growth remains stable and broadly in line with trend (2.7% – 2.8% y/y in real terms)
  • Around 75bps of additional rate cuts from the Fed and a core CPI/PCE fall of roughly 40–60 bps by year-end
  • The US dollar edges higher, and yields stay broadly flat.

But, as history shows, the macroeconomy rarely follows the path that market consensus dictates.

As such, we analyse the conditions that would push gold moderately higher (a shallow slip), significantly higher (the doom loop), and those that would prompt a notable pullback (reflation return).

A shallow slip

US economic data has been mixed, but market participants are concerned that momentum may be slowing. As risk appetite declines, positioning shifts to defensive assets.

Within this environment, a potential reset in AI expectations could act as an additional drag on equity markets, especially since AI names carry significant weight in major indices, amplifying market volatility and encouraging further de-risking.

This may result in a softer US labour market as record-high margins contract, which would prompt weaker consumer activity and contribute to a broader global growth slowdown.

Against this backdrop, the Fed would likely cut rates beyond current expectations, easing policy in response to rising economic uncertainty and expectations of cooling inflation.

Impact on gold: moderately bullish

The combination of lower interest rates and a weaker dollar paired with heightened risk aversion would create a continued supportive environment for gold.

Our analysis shows that, in this environment, gold could rise 5% - 15% in 2026 from current levels,5 depending on the severity of the economic slowdown and the speed and magnitude of the rate cuts.6

This would represent a solid return in a normal year, but following 2025’s strong performance, it would still be considered a noteworthy follow-up.

The combination of lower interest rates and a weaker dollar – both of which remain cyclically high (Chart 5) – have historically been a source of support for gold.

In addition, continued strategic central bank buying and potential new investment entrants, such as insurance companies in China or pension funds in India,7 could further support gold’s positive trend even if the economic environment remains relatively benign.

Chart 5: Real rates and the US dollar remain cyclically high

US dollar REER and US 10-year TIP yield*

The doom loop

There is a non-zero chance that the global economy moves into a deeper and more synchronised slowdown,8 driven by rising geopolitical and geoeconomic risk. Tensions around trade, unresolved regional conflicts, or a new flashpoint may erode confidence and weigh heavily on global activity. These pressures would contribute to a more fragmented global environment and heighten risk sensitivity across trade and investment.

As confidence fades, businesses scale back investment and households pull back on spending, setting off a self-reinforcing “doom loop” that deepens the downturn. US growth weakens further, and inflation falls below target, prompting the Fed to cut rates aggressively. Long-term yields decline sharply, and the US dollar softens as policy eases, contributing to softer global trade and broad commodity weakness.

Impact on gold: bullish

This combination of falling yields, elevated geopolitical stress and a pronounced flight-to-safety would create exceptionally strong tailwinds for gold, supporting a sharp move higher. Under this scenario, gold could surge 15% – 30% in 2026 from current levels.9

Investment demand, particularly via gold ETFs would remain a key driver, offsetting weakness in other areas of the market, such as jewellery or technology.

Rising prices have historically spurred investor interest, accelerating momentum. Global gold ETFs have seen US$77bn of inflows so far this year, adding more than 700t to their holdings.10 Even if we move the starting point back further to May 2024, collective gold ETF holdings are up by approximately 850t. This figure is less than half of what we have seen in previous gold bull cycles, leaving ample room for growth (Chart 6).

Chart 6: Investment flows during gold’s current bull run remain below those from previous cycles

Global gold ETF holdings and total fund flow*

Reflation return

On the flip side, there’s also a possibility that the policies set by the Trump administration succeed, resulting in stronger-than-expected growth linked to fiscal induced support. Under these conditions, reflation likely takes hold, pushing activity higher and lifting global growth toward a firmer trajectory. As inflation pressures mount, the Fed would be forced to hold or even hike rates in 2026.

This, in turn, would push long-term yields higher and strengthen the US dollar. The rise in yields and a firmer currency increase the opportunity cost of holding gold and draw capital back toward US assets. Improving economic sentiment would also fuel a broad risk-on rotation.

Impact on gold: bearish

Rising yields, a stronger dollar, and the shift toward risk-on positioning weigh heavily on gold, prompting a notable withdrawal of investor interest. With hedges unwound and retail demand softening, the backdrop turns decidedly negative, resulting in a gold price correction of between 5% and 20%, from current levels.11

Gold ETF holdings could see sustained outflows as investors rotate into equities and higher-yielding assets. Their magnitude would be a function of the reduction in gold’s risk-induced premium, which has been a mainstay since the invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

However, historical analysis also shows that opportunistic buying from consumers and long-term investors could act as a buffer in this kind of environment.

Despite this, the combination of higher opportunity costs, risk-on sentiment, and negative price momentum could create challenging conditions for gold, reinforcing this as the most bearish scenario in our outlook.

Wildcards

Beyond the scenarios outlined above, central bank demand and recycling supply are notable wildcards. These factors sit outside our traditional quantitative modelling for a few reasons but could materially influence gold markets.

Central bank demand remains a significant contributor to gold’s performance. Official sector purchases have been strong, and there are good reasons to expect central bank buying to continue.

Chart 7: Gold reserves from emerging markets are well below those from advanced economies

Gold as a share of total foreign reserves*

Gold reserves from emerging market countries, which are the main source of demand, remain well below those from developed countries (Chart 7). If geopolitical tensions escalate, EM purchases could accelerate, reinforcing structural support for gold.

Chart 8: Central bank demand has been an important contributor to gold’s performance

Actual and estimated annual central bank demand*

However, the decision process for central bank gold buying is often dictated by policy rather than market conditions alone. A significant pullback in purchases to or below pre-COVID levels could create additional headwinds for gold (Chart 8).

Recycling flows could also become a significant swing factor. Recycling has been relatively muted this year after accounting for factors such as the rise in the gold price and the effect of economic growth. This phenomenon has been linked to a notable increase in the use of gold as collateral for loans.

In India, consumers have pledged more than 200t of gold jewellery through the formal sector this year alone (Chart 9). And anecdotal evidence suggests there is almost as much gold backing loans from the informal sector.

If recycling remains subdued, with gold being used as collateral instead, it will continue to provide support. But a marked economic slowdown in India could trigger forced liquidations of gold-backed collateral, boosting secondary supply and adding pressure to prices. And while there is a widespread positive perspective for India’s economy, a severe global downturn – such as the Doom loop scenario – could create a spillover effect.

Chart 9: Loans with gold as collateral are on the rise

Estimated gold jewellery pledged as collateral in India and global recycled supply per quarter*

Conclusion

Gold’s outlook for 2026 is being defined by the uncertain economic environment that investors currently face. And, just like 2025, the upcoming year may bring significant volatility across financial markets.

While the current gold price broadly reflects the prevailing macroeconomic consensus and suggests a range-bound performance, our analysis indicates that the forces of softer growth, accommodative policy, and persistent geopolitical risks are more likely to support gold than to undermine it.

Moreover, gold investment, which has been critical to this year’s performance, still has room to grow.

Despite the plausibility of a bearish scenario, it is likely that investors will maintain some exposure to gold given the unpredictability of current geoeconomic dynamics.

In addition to investment demand, central banks and recycling can provide additional support. But, under certain conditions, they can also become headwinds.

Ultimately, the diversity of possible outcomes highlights the value of scenario-based planning. In a world where shocks and surprises are increasingly the norm, gold’s capacity to provide diversification and downside protection remains as relevant as ever.

Table 2: Gold responds to a combination of factors that influence its role as an asset

Hypothetical macroeconomic scenarios and their implied gold performance for 2026*

data.title: SPEM: Heavy Bet On Emerging Asia Ex. South Korea
data.description:
SPDR Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF Opportunity Overview

The SPDR Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF (SPEM) is a relatively unique vehicle that provides stronger exposure to some of the dominant emerging Asian economies, including China, India, and Taiwan.

This approach has led to substantial outperformance in the long run, even amid concerns over China's stock market and economy. If you want concentrated exposure to emerging Asia, this is a favorable vehicle to consider.

However, 2025 performance is also worth noting, as there were many surprise victors in the market, including South Korea and Brazil, which trounced other emerging markets. Herein lies the main downfall of this ETF, as it is not positioned to significantly benefit from rallies in some of the smaller constituent emerging markets.

2026 looks like a solid year for many emerging markets, with key Asian markets like Taiwan and China poised to benefit from increased AI demand. This ETF looks like an acceptable addition to an emerging market portfolio, with notable differentiation from the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, but it still excludes a lot of larger victors and other smaller underdog emerging markets that have a lot of potential this decade.

The SPDR Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF looks like a solid hold at the moment, even though I have some concerns about broader geopolitical and economic risks, as well as valuations in key markets like Taiwan and India.

Portfolio and Benchmark Index Overview

The SPDR Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF is an exchange-traded fund that tracks some of the dominant emerging markets, including China, Taiwan, and India. It charges a 0.07% fee and tracks the S&P Emerging BMI Index.

SPEM has over 3,000 holdings with an average market cap of $159 billion. This ETF is a relatively attractive value play, as it trades at circa 15x earnings and 2x book value, a slight discount to the MSCI EM Index.

Even though it primarily invests in larger companies, the SPDR Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF still offers a circa 2.5% dividend yield.

The ETF's benchmark index has some unique features relative to the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, a common benchmark for many emerging market ETFs. The index chooses emerging market companies based on a variety of characteristics, including the market cap size and 6-12 month median trading value.

S&P Emerging BMI Index

This index primarily focuses on information technology and financial companies in leading emerging markets, with China, Taiwan, and India accounting for nearly 75% of the entire index. This index is around 10 percentage points overweight in these markets relative to the MSCI Emerging Markets Index.

The SPDR Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF efficiently tracks this benchmark index and is consequently a superior bet for those who are more bullish on dominant Asian emerging markets.

As seen below, its main holdings mirror the index, with a maximum of a circa 30 basis points difference relative to the index’s top holdings.

SPEM Top Holdings

This ETF has a slightly higher weighting in IT and financial stocks, which have been some of the top-performing industries in emerging markets. Both of these industries collectively account for 44.2% of its assets.

Heavily focused on Asia, ex. South Korea: Its country approach is also in line with its benchmark index, as its top three constituents account for around 73% of its total assets. It is also underweight markets like Brazil, which make up a large % of the MSCI EM Index. This concentrated approach is good for Asia bulls, as it provides exposure to some of the dominant Asian economies, excluding South Korea, but leaves the ETF vulnerable to regional pullbacks.

Emerging Asia Potential

These markets have ample long-term growth potential, but still lag behind other dominant EM names. This is a weird setup, especially since South Korea and Brazil are top 5 MSCI EM constituents and have a favorable outlook ahead of them.

This underperformance comes at a time when valuations in markets like India and Taiwan have become stretched, while Brazil and South Korea trade at lower valuations.

P/E P/B Dividend Yield
MSCI India Index 26.9 3.8 1.2%
MSCI Taiwan index 23.1 4.1 2.1%
MSCI Brazil Index 9.4 1.7 6.0%
MSCI South Korea Index 17.1 1.6 1.4%

Source: MSCI's latest available data

China's Growth Prospects Are Favorable

China is still well worth considering as a value play, although its higher weighting is a source of concern. The IMF recently upgraded its growth potential for China to 4.8% next year, in line with its 2020-2023 average.

An Irrational Discount to Emerging Markets

At the moment, China is still attractively priced given its dominant standing in AI and other technological innovations. MSCI China still trades at a slight discount to the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, despite this standing.

Chinese equities are also trading at a massive discount to US equities, which have become inflated in recent years.

Taiwan's New Record Growth

As AI investments continue to ramp up, Taiwan has positioned its economy in a very favorable way. The country's economic growth is poised to hit a 15-year high on the back of record AI demand.

India Economic Outlook and Valuation Concerns

India, which accounts for nearly 20% of this ETF's assets, has a very favorable outlook, but is one of the pricier emerging markets. However, India still has one of the strongest growth outlooks in the world at the moment, and looks like a solid hold.

Risks and Other Additions to Note

One of the main factors to note is that it is possible for investors to access the market at even lower valuations by avoiding markets like India and Taiwan, and going overweight other markets in Asia and South America. For example, Brazil and South Korea are still compelling value plays at the moment.

SPEM is heavily exposed to some of the economic risks in the Asia region, and particularly to any potential China and Taiwan tensions, given its 50+% weighting in these countries.

This vehicle is still unique from other emerging market funds, and offers reasonable valuation despite its exposure to more expensive Asian markets. It has also been a long-term outperformer of the MSCI EM Index.

I give this ETF a hold rating, especially since Chinese equities seem reasonably priced despite the risk, and it invests circa 30% of its assets in other promising emerging markets. However, I think its choice to exclude other dominant and promising value plays makes other emerging market ETFs better picks.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: Dividend Cut Unlikely In 2026
data.description:
My Long Thesis For Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL)

With interest rates falling and many BDCs sensitive to falling interest rates, the risk of dividend cuts is looming over the industry at large. One BDC that I deem to be quite insulated from a dividend cut is Blackstone Secured Lending which has recently seen a contraction in its spread between net investment income and dividend distributions in the last year, but which still covers its dividend at a rate of more than 100% (as of Q3-2025).

Plus, available undistributed spillover income is a buffer against a short-term short-fall of GAAP NII. Blackstone Secured Lending’s BV multiple retreated to about 1.0x lately which culminates in a buy recommendation.

High-Quality Private Credit Pool Facing NII-Related Coverage Challenges

A number of BDCs have cut their dividends in 2025, the latest one being FS KKR Capital (FSK) which is the 5th largest BDC by a market valuation. FS KKR Capital has changed its dividend setup in light of performance challenges and high non-accruals in its private loan business.

The result: it will pay a lower $0.45 dividend in Q1-2026 and add a variable component of $0.10 to the payout. In future quarters, this variable dividend will depend on credit performance. The challenges faced by FS KKR, which had cost-based non-accruals of 5% in Q3-2025, underscores the need for you to pick the best of the best BDCs.

Blackstone Secured Lending is predominantly invested in first-liens and the BDC has earned a reputation amongst investors as a low-risk private credit platform, and for good measure: Blackstone Secured Lending had only 0.1% of its assets marked on non-accrual, meaning only a tiny sliver of its more than $13.8 billion in investments are seen as problematic.

Blackstone Secured Lending, if anything, is the exemplary BDC with an almost sole focus on senior secured debt. In Q3-2025 Blackstone Secured Lending had an almost exclusive investment focus on senior secured loans, making up 97.5% of the private credit pool’s investment allocation. A marginal percentage of assets, only 2.5%, related to investments other than first-liens, which for the most part were lower-ranked second-liens and some equity positions.

Associated with Blackstone Secured Lending’s first-lien profile is what I consider to be the BDC’s biggest vulnerability. In the graphic breakdown of the private loan business’s portfolio you can see that 98.6% of BXSL’s investments are in floating-rate debt. This, for obvious reasons, is a potential problem for the BDC as the market has started to price lower interest rates into asset prices.

In fact, crashing crypto currency prices, for example, indicate decreasing risk appetites and therefore suggest that the market is less willing to gamble on high-yielding credit vehicles. For Blackstone Secured Lending this could become a problem, especially if the BDC were to see a falling spread between its GAAP NII and its distribution.

First-Lien Portfolio, But Contracting NII Spread

Yet, despite a strong asset performance, there are risks for Blackstone Secured Lending and you should not assume that a high-quality BDC could not be forced to lower its payout if its loan performance does not support its payout. Blackstone Secured Lending generated $737 million in NII in Q3-2025 LTM, up 6% YoY, because of new loans made during this period.

Blackstone Secured Lending’s spread (the difference between its GAAP NII and regular distribution) is still positive and averaged $0.06 per share in the last four quarters. Only once in the last year did the dividend coverage ratio fall to 100% (in Q2-2025), but the ratio rebounded to 106.5% in Q3-2025.

The spread in Q3-2025, signalling the BDC’s excess income, was $0.05 in September 2025. The trend since Q124 shows that the degree of coverage nonetheless deteriorated which makes it imperative to look at the business’s spillover income.

Spillover income acts as a reserve account from which a BDC can pull funds down in order to make up a shortfall in GAAP NII. Blackstone Secured Lending’s spillover income in Q3-2025 was $1.89 per share.

This undistributed taxable income is income from prior periods that was retained on the business’s balance sheet. This excess income is a buffer for the BDC to prevent a dividend cut if current-quarter GAAP NII slips below Blackstone Secured Lending’s $0.77 distribution. The undistributed spillover income is equal to 61% of Blackstone Secured Lending’s total FWD dividend payout.

The spread, or safety margin, is narrowing and there is no guarantee that the BDC won’t cut its dividend if, for example, the Fed were to accelerate its rate cuts in 2026.

Fair Value Estimate

I prefer to acquire BDC yields at a decent-sized 10%-or-higher discounts to net asset value, for safety considerations. However, I am willing to pay full price (meaning BVPS) for a BDC’s potential and covered payout if the business has a history of strong asset quality and good loan performance. This is clearly the case with Blackstone Secured Lending which makes the BDC long-term buy-and-hold at 1.01 BV. The average BDC has a 0.9x BV multiple.

My game plan for BXSL looks as follows: I buy BXSL up to BVPS ($27.15) and maintain a buy rating. Below 0.9 BV ($24.44) I would upgrade my rating to strong buy. Above 1.1 BV ($30.00) I would downgrade BXSL to hold only.

The only BDCs that are getting a premium to BV in the market are Blackstone Secured Lending and Ares Capital which mainly relates to strong long-term asset performance, resulting in high trust that the dividend will be maintained.

Lastly, from an asset profile setup, Blackstone Secured Lending has the highest first-lien investment percentage and lowest cost-based non-accrual ratio in the peer group, making it potentially the best high-yield investment among BXSL’s comparables.

Risk Analysis

If Blackstone Secured Lending’s NII were to slip below the business’s 77 cents per share distribution, I would likely start to get a bit more antsy. The trend in coverage, in my opinion, unfortunately is not the best and the spread has declined since Q1-2024, though risen QoQ in Q3-2025.

Though I don’t expect BXSL to cut its dividend in 2026, given its more than sufficient spillover account, there might be a risk for the BDC that originates from other private loan businesses.

If one floating-rate BDC were to cut its dividend, many others may follow in its wake, potentially creating a cascading effect in the BDC sector.

My Verdict: Buy

The current situation for Blackstone Secured Lending does not indicate that a dividend cut is probable.

The private loan business covers its payout fully with NII and the BDC has undistributed spillover income equal to 61% of a full year’s FWD dividend payout. Insufficient NII is currently not a problem, neither is asset quality. The last GAAP NII to distribution spread was positive 6.5 cents and the $1.89 per share in spillover income provides considerable protection.

All of these things lead me to believe that a dividend cut in 2026 is highly unlikely, even under consideration that Blackstone Secured Lending has a high floating-rate percentage of 98.6% and interest rates are set to fall next year.

data.title: BAI: A Higher-Conviction AI Play Built For The Next Market Phase
data.description:
The AI ETFs have generally not been around for decades to check performance across cycles. Current analysis becomes restricted to performance comparisons in unidirectional bull markets since 2023 (with a sharp but short correction in April 2025). When analyzing iShares A.I. Innovation and Tech Active ETF (BAI), I will obviously rely on performance trends, but try to dive into potential repercussions in market types different from today's mega-cap dominated and bull conditions. The fact that BAI is active makes that task harder, because BAI may actively change its structural biases evident now. And of course, the fact remains that the AI secular tailwinds are real, and analyzing for bearish scenarios may be a low probability event.

Overall, BAI does look a decent bet on the AI theme that could do well within a basket of AI ETFs. It does well from a growth ETF perspective in bull markets, but drawdowns look sharper compared to some passive strategies. Within the active plays, BAI has some better alternatives, but has the potential to do well in the right market conditions. Overall, BAI is a buy - investors should expect it to be a higher risk higher returns play within the AI ETF alternatives.

Portfolio Insights and Fund Characteristics

The methodology here is active. It focuses on companies that meet current or forecasted revenues or net income thresholds and are projected to be future market leaders in terms of enablement, development, utilization, and/or deployment of AI technology and products. Since the methodology is active, I have presented the sectoral allocations and the top 10 holdings data below relative to that of CHAT - the AI ETF which has pulled away from the crowd and outperformed significantly since April 2025.

CHAT, with a focus on generative AI bets heavily on tech and communications (almost 94% of the portfolio). BAI has a slightly heavier focus on AI-led disruptions in other sectors (~10%). Pure tech and communications is slightly lower at 90%. BAI leans more heavily towards chips, software and infrastructure (classified as technology), where CHAT is more heavily weighted in the platform companies (Meta, Alphabet) that are leaders in developing and deploying Generative AI models.

For two similar sized portfolios, BAI appears to be the more high conviction and concentrated one. In BAI's case, the higher weightage to Broadcom and the inclusion of Celestica and Fabrinet (AI services/manufacturing/supply chain) confirms its commitment to the services and infrastructure layers, particularly the industrials exposure.

Since BAI categorizes its holdings using the following layers - A.I. power, Accelerated computing, Cloud infrastructure, A.I. models, Data, Data tools/infrastructure software, A.I. applications, A.I. services and solutions - I took a look at the detailed full holdings of both the ETFs and broadly categorized them into these buckets.

CHAT and BAI show similar allocations broadly - the distinct approaches have a marginal variation between portfolios, thematically speaking. CHAT appears more geographically and thematically diversified. It owns chips and memory (SK Hynix, Samsung, TSMC, ASML), GPUs and accelerators, cloud infrastructure (Microsoft, Amazon, Google, Oracle, Dell) and AI models (Meta, Palantir, SenseTime, iFlytek). This balances exposure to different parts of the AI ecosystem. BAI also is spread across, but is relatively more focused on high-powered computing and cloud platforms, with heavier allocations to NVIDIA, Broadcom, and US hyperscalers.

Since BAI is an active strategy, its turnover characteristics are important to discuss too. Compared to CHAT, BAI appears to be more conservative there with a turnover of 56% (compared to CHAT's 92%). This implies agility to adapt to market changes, but also sufficient patience to allocated high conviction bets to play out. The lower turnover also reflects in its lower expense ratio of 0.55% compared to CHAT's 0.75%.

Versus Active Peers

The performance (total returns) show slight underperformance in BAI compared to CHAT. ARKQ is an extreme active end of the active innovation theme and has outperformed significantly in the period of analysis. Between CHAT and BAI, in flat to correcting markets, BAI often outperforms CHAT (although marginally). The bulk of CHAT's recent outperformance has been in a unidirectional bull market since April 2025. A part of this data can be explained by the fact that CHAT's diversified AI play ensures better returns when AI rallies are strong and spill over to names beyond US hyperscalers. The same US mega-cap bias in BAI could turn out to be more defensive in consolidating markets.

Merely looking at empirical data, despite the noted differences depending on market conditions, it looks like CHAT holds up reasonably well even in flat to correcting markets, while outperforming in rallies. A deeper dive into performance attribution reveals far stronger performance in the top 10 in the case of BAI. BAI's top 10 account for ~50% of the portfolio and generated ~60% returns from April. The bottom half of the portfolio (more small and mid cap names) underperformed the top half - contributing only 17% of the 77% returns overall.

For CHAT, the top 10 accounts for ~44% of the portfolio by weight and going by the individual stock returns in the top 10, the heavyweights account for only 42% returns since April. The overall portfolio returns are 97% though. In other words, CHAT's remaining portfolio (primarily smaller caps and mid-caps) outperformed significantly and contributed a healthy 55% of the 97% returns. That is, the overall portfolio saw homogeneous performance.

CHAT’s smaller holdings were on high-conviction bets on the companies that build and power AI hardware and cloud infrastructure. These included specialized AI server providers (Super Micro Computer), AI chip designers (ARM), and emerging AI cloud services (CoreWeave). They were sensitive to momentum plays. When the AI rally took off, speculative capital poured into every stock related to the supply chain ecosystem, not just the large chipmakers. Because CHAT’s small and mid cap holdings were essential, pure-play enablers, they experienced exponential price appreciation (multi-bagger returns) that the larger, more diversified stocks could not match.

Versus Passive

The debate between active and passive is sensitive to market environments too, but I feel agility to react to changing trends are the need of the hour as the AI landscape evolves. Since most passive plays (charted below) are heavily market cap biased, they often will react after market changes are absorbed in share prices. However, the active plays also become higher risk in nature (for the relatively higher growth phases on average). In the April sell-off, BAI crashed much more than most of the passive plays.

Below are some outside alternatives to BAI when it comes to AI investment avenues. QQQ or even XLK are not worse off when adjusted for risk (lower drawdowns). But if pure play AI theme is the bet, BAI has consistently outperformed these diversified and passive broad alternatives.

BAI is a Buy

Despite a stronger empirical performance in alternatives (like CHAT particularly), I find value in BAI. BAI gets large caps "very, very right", even compared to CHAT. This means better ability to navigate downturns or flatter markets or when the AI play starts identifying winners over a broad-based rally at the moment. My outlook is that this selective AI play will be a reality in the second leg of the rally.

CHAT’s smaller, high-momentum holdings are highly volatile and will likely suffer sharp corrections if the AI rally stalls. BAI’s broader, more balanced portfolio, which holds many essential AI service integrators and less-speculative names, is inherently more defensive. In a period of consolidation or a general market downturn, BAI's diversification will act as a buffer, allowing it to hold up better than CHAT. Even if the AI rally continues but rewards chosen "monetization, essentials, reasonably valued" themes over narratives (as I expect), BAI should do very well.

data.title: Impax Global Sustainable Infrastructure Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

  • The portfolio underperformed its FTSE Global Infrastructure benchmark during the quarter. A strong recovery across global equity markets has not benefitted the portfolio’s defensive growth tilt. At a headline level, the portfolio’s positions in companies that are enjoying rapid growth driven by structural trends, such as data centres and electrical grid spending, have performed strongly. Conversely, stocks with more stable growth profiles, such as waste management and water utilities, have given up some of their returns.
  • Company results have also played a significant role in driving performance over the quarter. At a time of relatively elevated valuations and geopolitical uncertainty, even tame earnings updates seem capable of provoking double-digit moves. The portfolio has thus benefitted strongly from positive updates from the likes of KLA and Welltower, while seeing equally negative moves in companies such as Chemed.
  • Stock selection and sector allocation both detracted over the period, with currency effects having a neutral impact. Stock selection was strongest in the Real Estate and Information Technology (IT) sectors. However, this was offset by weaker picks in Industrials and Utilities. In terms of sector allocation, the portfolio benefitted from its overweight to Financials. Overweight positions in Real Estate and Health Care made significantly negative contributions to performance.

Market overview

  • Global equities posted strong gains in Q3 2025, supported by resilient macroeconomic conditions, improving corporate earnings, fiscal stimulus and deregulation in the banking sector. However, market dynamics favoring hypergrowth, cyclical and value stocks have created headwinds for investors focused on quality, and market concentration increased over the period.
  • The passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill (OBBB) in the US was a key catalyst early in the quarter, providing short-term stimulus through extended tax cuts and incentives for domestic reshoring. While concerns linger over its longer-term impact on the US fiscal deficit and inflation, the OBBB helped lift investor sentiment, particularly in large-cap and technology stocks. The US Federal Reserve’s September interest rate cut also helped to support share prices and extend the risk-on rally in equities.
  • Emerging markets also performed well, buoyed by a weaker US dollar and supportive policy measures in China, which included new renewable energy standards targeting high-emission industries and support for the technology sector.
  • As we end Q3, US regulatory uncertainty has eased for Health Care stocks with the announcement of a deal between President Trump and drug company Pfizer (PFE) to lower prescription drug prices for the government-funded Medicaid program. In the short term, the US government shutdown may weigh on investor sentiment but historically these shutdowns have been resolved with little impact on equity markets.
  • Despite elevated valuations and increased market concentration, the investment team believes opportunities exist in stocks that have been de-rated despite strong earnings momentum and among companies demonstrating strong secular growth. The investment team remains focused on identifying businesses with durable growth drivers, sound management and attractive relative valuations.

Contributors

  • Tele2 (OTCPK:TLTZF)(Communications & Data, Sweden) made the largest positive contribution to performance. Shares in the Swedish telecoms provider rallied after management reported a significant Q2 earnings beat. The company has made faster-than-expected cost reductions under the new Iliad-led management team and is also delivering robust revenue growth in the Baltic region.
  • Welltower (WELL)(Healthcare, US) also boosted portfolio performance. Shares in the healthcare real estate investment trust (REIT) rose following strong quarterly results, notably in senior housing where occupancy and margins were both ahead of expectations. Management is adding capacity through acquisitions and increased the dividend. Successive updates from the company suggest that the pace of expansion is being sustained.
  • KLA (KLAC)(Water, US) further lifted returns. The specialist provider of semiconductor manufacturing tools and services reported results which came in ahead of expectations and saw guidance raised. The latter is notably ahead of competitors. KLA has been a strong beneficiary from continued investment in AI-related technology, with share price performance to match. As a result, we are monitoring its valuation closely.

Detractors

  • American Tower (AMT)(Communications & Data, US) made the largest negative contribution to performance. Shares in the broadcast tower REIT weakened following news that one of its customers – EchoStar – which accounts for roughly 2% of revenue, will terminate agreements after spectrum sales. With the company having recently increased its FY25 guidance, American Tower remains one of the strongest companies in its peer group.
  • Voltronic (Energy, Taiwan) also declined over the quarter. The supplier of power management solutions has been weak on the back of declining demand for its power storage and solar inverter business. On power storage, demand was impacted by tariff concerns and a weak macroeconomic environment. For its solar inverter business, rising competition from China has impacted both growth and margin outlook.
  • Sika (OTCPK:SXYAY)(Buildings & Facilities, Switzerland) similarly detracted from performance. The Swiss supplier of construction chemicals missed sales expectations amid currency pressures and weaker China demand. While local currency growth and margin improvements helped, overall revenue declined. Management lowered full-year guidance, citing macroeconomic uncertainty. Strategic acquisitions and expansions continue, but its near-term outlook is cautious.

GICS sector attribution#

  • The portfolio underperformed its FTSE Global Infrastructure benchmark. Stock selection and sector allocation both detracted over the period, with currency effects having a neutral impact.
  • Stock selection was strongest in the Real Estate and IT sectors. However, this was offset by weaker picks in Industrials and Utilities. In terms of sector allocation, the portfolio benefitted from its overweight to Financials. Overweight positions in Real Estate and Health Care made significant negative contributions to performance.

Outlook

  • Global equities, as measured by the MSCI ACWI Index, stood at all-time highs at the end of Q3. President Trump’s onslaught of tariffs has not instigated a massive surge in US inflation, and American consumers remain resilient thus far. US government bond 10-year yields are at 4.1%, down from their peak of 4.8% in January. Even the long-awaited resurgence in M&A appears to have arrived, with 2025 set to be the best year for mergers since 2021.
  • Against this rosy picture must be balanced some less positive data. Seven AI-linked companies now account for over 20% of the ACWI’s market cap. Gold – historically viewed as a safe haven asset – also now trades at all-time highs. From a macroeconomic standpoint, US inflation remains above the Federal Reserve’s target rate. Meanwhile unemployment is ticking up, with a US government shutdown only muddying the picture by preventing the publication of jobs numbers. Globally, yields on long-dated bonds have surged, as investors question long-term fiscal deficits.
  • The managers believe that an allocation to the strategy is more than justified in this environment. Year to date, the portfolio managers have taken advantage of market volatility to initiate positions in companies harnessing long-term structural growth at attractive valuations such as Keppel DC, the Singapore-listed data centre REIT. Market concentration has also enabled the team to buy positions in undervalued defensive compounders, such as US industrial gases group Linde. With an active share of more than 88%, meaningfully higher expected earnings growth than its FTSE Infrastructure benchmark, and a price-to-earnings ((PE)) premium of only 19%, the team is confident that the portfolio is well positioned heading into Q4.

The Ex-Ante Beta is calculated using a multi-factor risk model. Beta explains common variations in stock returns due to different stock sensitivities to the market relative to its underlying benchmark for the current period, not historical. A beta for a benchmark is 1.00: A beta greater than 1.00 indicates above average volatility and risk.

The FTSE Global Infrastructure Opportunities Index is designed to reflect the performance of infrastructure and infrastructure-related listed securities worldwide. Companies must derive a minimum of 20% of their revenue from either the core infrastructure activities or the infrastructure-related activities to be considered for index inclusion. The Index weights its constituents according to their investable market capitalization (after the application of free float and foreign ownership restrictions) in the index calculation. The FTSE Global Infrastructure Opportunities Index does not take account of ESG factors in its index construction. Base currency is in US Dollars (USD). For further information, please visit https://www.ftserussell.com/products/indices/infra.

The MSCI All Country World Index is a free float-adjusted market capitalization weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets. It covers 23 developed and 24 emerging market country indexes. The index is displayed in USD.

Environmental, social, and governance (ESG) criteria are a set of standards for a company’s operations that socially conscious investors use to screen potential investments. Social criteria examine how it manages relationships with employees, suppliers, customers, and the communities where it operates. Governance deals with a company’s leadership, executive pay, audits, internal controls, and shareholder rights.
